narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite challenges like your lower-middle family income background, and we appreciate the unique perspective you bring. The admissions model evaluated your profile by predicting your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 56% chance of failure—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 is solid and close to the typical student's 3.21 average, while your part-time enrollment status (uncommon, as most students go full-time) actually worked in your favor, suggesting better odds of passing compared to full-timers. However, your LSAT score of 20 stood out as the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure—it's well below the average of 36.7, making it the most influential reason for the model's concern. Your Year 1 school decile of 1 (far below the average of 5.71) and Year 3 decile of 2 (below the 5.53 average) also contributed toward the failure prediction, indicating early academic struggles relative to peers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA pulled strongly in the opposite direction, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass, as did your family income and part-time plans. Compared to typical students, your grades started weaker but your GPA held up decently, though the low LSAT overshadowed these strengths and set you apart from the stronger scorers who usually get approved. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, as it's the top lever here, and perhaps seek targeted support for early-grade building if reapplying. Strengthening these areas could flip the model's outlook next time, opening doors for talented women like you in law. We're rooting for your success.
